question:
B-3. Show that if the series
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{p_{n}}
\]
is convergent, where \( p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, \cdots, p_{n}, \cdots \) are positive real numbers, then the series
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{2}}{\left(p_{1}+p_{2}+\cdots+p_{n}\right)^{2}} p_{n}
\]
is also convergent.
solution:
B-3 Set \( q_{n}=p_{1}+p_{2}+\cdots+p_{n}\left(q_{0}=0\right) \). We are led to estimate \( S_{N} \) \( =\sum_{x=1}^{N}\left(n / q_{n}\right)^{2}\left(q_{n}-q_{n-1}\right) \) in terms of \( T=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1 / p_{n} \). Note that
\[
\begin{aligned}
S_{N} & \leqq \frac{1}{p_{1}}+\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n^{2}}{q_{n} q_{n-1}}\left(q_{n}-q_{n-1}\right)=\frac{1}{p_{1}}+\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n^{2}}{q_{n-1}}-\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n^{2}}{q_{n}} \\
& =\frac{1}{p_{1}}+\sum_{n=1}^{N-1} \frac{(n+1)^{2}}{q_{n}}-\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n^{2}}{q_{n}} \leqq \frac{5}{p_{1}}+2 \sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n}{q_{n}}+\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{1}{q_{n}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By Schwarz's inequality,
\[
\left(\sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n}{q_{n}}\right)^{2} \leqq \sum_{n=2}^{N} \frac{n^{2}}{q_{n}^{2}} p_{n} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{p_{n}}
\]
and thus
\[
S_{N} \leqq \frac{5}{p_{1}}+2 \sqrt{S_{N} T}+T
\]

This quadratic inequality implies that \( \sqrt{S_{N}} \leqq \sqrt{T}+\sqrt{2 T+5 / p_{1}} \).

descriptive_long.question:
B-3. Show that if the series
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{seqelem}
\]
is convergent, where \( firstelem, secondelem, thirdelem, \cdots, seqelem, \cdots \) are positive real numbers, then the series
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{\left(firstelem+secondelem+\cdots+seqelem\right)^{2}} seqelem
\]
is also convergent.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3 Set \( partialsum=firstelem+secondelem+\cdots+seqelem\left(zeropartial=0\right) \). We are led to estimate \( partialseries \) \( =\sum_{summand=1}^{uppernn}\left(indexvar / partialsum\right)^{2}\left(partialsum-previouspartial\right) \) in terms of \( totalsum=\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} 1 / seqelem \). Note that
\[
\begin{aligned}
partialseries & \leqq \frac{1}{firstelem}+\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{partialsum\,previouspartial}\left(partialsum-previouspartial\right)=\frac{1}{firstelem}+\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{previouspartial}-\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{partialsum} \\
& =\frac{1}{firstelem}+\sum_{indexvar=1}^{uppernn-1} \frac{(indexvar+1)^{2}}{partialsum}-\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{partialsum} \leqq \frac{5}{firstelem}+2 \sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar}{partialsum}+\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{1}{partialsum} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By Schwarz's inequality,
\[
\left(\sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar}{partialsum}\right)^{2} \leqq \sum_{indexvar=2}^{uppernn} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{partialsum^{2}} seqelem \sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{seqelem}
\]
and thus
\[
partialseries \leqq \frac{5}{firstelem}+2 \sqrt{partialseries\, totalsum}+totalsum
\]

This quadratic inequality implies that \( \sqrt{partialseries} \leqq \sqrt{totalsum}+\sqrt{2\, totalsum+5 / firstelem} \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-3. Show that if the series
\[
\sum_{pineapple=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{marigolds}
\]
is convergent, where \( jellyfish, blackbird, rainstorm, \cdots, marigolds, \cdots \) are positive real numbers, then the series
\[
\sum_{pineapple=1}^{\infty} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{\left(jellyfish+blackbird+\cdots+marigolds\right)^{2}} marigolds
\]
is also convergent.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3 Set \( snowflake=jellyfish+blackbird+\cdots+marigolds\left(treasurer=0\right) \). We are led to estimate \( horseshoe \) \( =\sum_{tangerine=1}^{toothbrush}\left(pineapple / snowflake\right)^{2}\left(snowflake-lemonade\right) \) in terms of \( whisperer=\sum_{pineapple=1}^{\infty} 1 / marigolds \). Note that
\[
\begin{aligned}
horseshoe & \leqq \frac{1}{jellyfish}+\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{snowflake \, lemonade}\left(snowflake-lemonade\right)=\frac{1}{jellyfish}+\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{lemonade}-\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{snowflake} \\
& =\frac{1}{jellyfish}+\sum_{pineapple=1}^{toothbrush-1} \frac{(pineapple+1)^{2}}{snowflake}-\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{snowflake} \leqq \frac{5}{jellyfish}+2 \sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple}{snowflake}+\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{1}{snowflake} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By Schwarz's inequality,
\[
\left(\sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple}{snowflake}\right)^{2} \leqq \sum_{pineapple=2}^{toothbrush} \frac{pineapple^{2}}{snowflake^{2}} marigolds \sum_{pineapple=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{marigolds}
\]
and thus
\[
horseshoe \leqq \frac{5}{jellyfish}+2 \sqrt{horseshoe \, whisperer}+whisperer
\]

This quadratic inequality implies that \( \sqrt{horseshoe} \leqq \sqrt{whisperer}+\sqrt{2 \, whisperer+5 / jellyfish} \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-3. Show that if the series
\[
\sum_{finishline=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{negativeseq_{finishline}}
\]
is convergent, where \( negseqfirst, negseqsecond, negseqthird, \cdots, negativeseq_{finishline}, \cdots \) are positive real numbers, then the series
\[
\sum_{finishline=1}^{\infty} \frac{finishline^{2}}{\left(negseqfirst+negseqsecond+\cdots+negativeseq_{finishline}\right)^{2}} \, negativeseq_{finishline}
\]
is also convergent.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3 Set \( differencevar_{finishline}=negseqfirst+negseqsecond+\cdots+negativeseq_{finishline}\left(differencestart=0\right) \). We are led to estimate \( gapaggregate \) \( =\sum_{stillness=1}^{originpoint}\left(finishline / differencevar_{finishline}\right)^{2}\left(differencevar_{finishline}-differenceprev\right) \) in terms of \( singlevalue=\sum_{finishline=1}^{\infty} 1 / negativeseq_{finishline} \). Note that
\[
\begin{aligned}
gapaggregate & \leqq \frac{1}{negseqfirst}+\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline^{2}}{differencevar_{finishline}\,differenceprev}\left(differencevar_{finishline}-differenceprev\right)=\frac{1}{negseqfirst}+\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline^{2}}{differenceprev}-\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline^{2}}{differencevar_{finishline}} \\
& =\frac{1}{negseqfirst}+\sum_{finishline=1}^{originpoint-1} \frac{(finishline+1)^{2}}{differencevar_{finishline}}-\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline^{2}}{differencevar_{finishline}} \leqq \frac{5}{negseqfirst}+2 \sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline}{differencevar_{finishline}}+\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{1}{differencevar_{finishline}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By Schwarz's inequality,
\[
\left(\sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline}{differencevar_{finishline}}\right)^{2} \leqq \sum_{finishline=2}^{originpoint} \frac{finishline^{2}}{differencevar_{finishline}^{2}} \, negativeseq_{finishline} \, \sum_{finishline=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{negativeseq_{finishline}}
\]
and thus
\[
gapaggregate \leqq \frac{5}{negseqfirst}+2 \sqrt{gapaggregate \, singlevalue}+singlevalue
\]

This quadratic inequality implies that \( \sqrt{gapaggregate} \leqq \sqrt{singlevalue}+\sqrt{2 \, singlevalue+5 / negseqfirst} \).

garbled_string.question:
B-3. Show that if the series
\[
\sum_{vzxlrkqa=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{epqtrmno}
\]
is convergent, where \( uavdkhzc, jrosqnel, abqlisef, \cdots, epqtrmno, \cdots \) are positive real numbers, then the series
\[
\sum_{vzxlrkqa=1}^{\infty} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{\left(uavdkhzc+jrosqnel+\cdots+epqtrmno\right)^{2}} epqtrmno
\]
is also convergent.
garbled_string.solution:
B-3 Set \( ulfwymte=uavdkhzc+jrosqnel+\cdots+epqtrmno\left(gksajfzi=0\right) \). We are led to estimate \( bnkohuds \) \( =\sum_{mfdjwhzs=1}^{tbqphkro}\left(vzxlrkqa / ulfwymte\right)^{2}\left(ulfwymte-wtbrmlas\right) \) in terms of \( rgtdycwe=\sum_{vzxlrkqa=1}^{\infty} 1 / epqtrmno \). Note that
\[
\begin{aligned}
bnkohuds & \leqq \frac{1}{uavdkhzc}+\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{ulfwymte wtbrmlas}\left(ulfwymte-wtbrmlas\right)=\frac{1}{uavdkhzc}+\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{wtbrmlas}-\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{ulfwymte} \\
& =\frac{1}{uavdkhzc}+\sum_{vzxlrkqa=1}^{tbqphkro-1} \frac{(vzxlrkqa+1)^{2}}{ulfwymte}-\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{ulfwymte} \leqq \frac{5}{uavdkhzc}+2 \sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa}{ulfwymte}+\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{1}{ulfwymte} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By Schwarz's inequality,
\[
\left(\sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa}{ulfwymte}\right)^{2} \leqq \sum_{vzxlrkqa=2}^{tbqphkro} \frac{vzxlrkqa^{2}}{ulfwymte^{2}} epqtrmno \sum_{vzxlrkqa=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{epqtrmno}
\]
and thus
\[
bnkohuds \leqq \frac{5}{uavdkhzc}+2 \sqrt{bnkohuds\, rgtdycwe}+rgtdycwe
\]

This quadratic inequality implies that \( \sqrt{bnkohuds} \leqq \sqrt{rgtdycwe}+\sqrt{2 rgtdycwe+5 / uavdkhzc} \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(p_n)_{n\ge 1}$ be a sequence of positive real numbers such that
\[\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{p_n}<\infty.\]
Denote the partial sums by $q_n=p_1+p_2+\dots+p_n\;(q_0:=0)$.  Prove that the series
\[\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n(n+1)}{q_n^{\,2}}\,p_n\]
converges.
kernel_variant.solution:
Let p_1,p_2,\ldots  be positive with \sum _n1/p_n=T<\infty , and set q_0=0, q_n=p_1+\cdots +p_n.  We must show the partial sums
  S_N=\sum _{n=1}^N n(n+1)\cdot p_n/q_n^2
stay bounded as N\to \infty .

1.  For n=1,
  1\cdot 2\cdot p_1/q_1^2=2p_1/p_1^2=2/p_1.

2.  For n\geq 2, q_n\geq q_{n-1}>0 implies 1/q_n^2 \leq 1/(q_n q_{n-1}).  Hence
  n(n+1)p_n/q_n^2=n(n+1)(q_n-q_{n-1})/q_n^2
    \leq n(n+1)(q_n-q_{n-1})/(q_n q_{n-1})
    =n(n+1)(1/q_{n-1}-1/q_n).
Thus
  S_N \leq 2/p_1 + \sum _{n=2}^N n(n+1)(1/q_{n-1}-1/q_n).

3.  Telescope the sum for n\geq 2.  Writing
  \sum _{n=2}^N n(n+1)/q_{n-1}-\sum _{n=2}^N n(n+1)/q_n
and shifting indices in the first sum shows it equals
  6/p_1 + \sum _{k=2}^{N-1}2(k+1)/q_k - N(N+1)/q_N.
Dropping the nonpositive last term gives
  \sum _{n=2}^N n(n+1)(1/q_{n-1}-1/q_n)
    \leq 6/p_1 +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}(k+1)/q_k
    =6/p_1 +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}k/q_k +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}1/q_k.

4.  Hence
  S_N \leq 2/p_1 +6/p_1 +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}k/q_k +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}1/q_k
       =8/p_1 +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}k/q_k +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}1/q_k.
Since q_k\geq p_k,  \sum 1/q_k \leq \sum 1/p_k=T, so
  S_N \leq 8/p_1 +2\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}k/q_k +2T.

5.  By Cauchy-Schwarz with a_k=k\sqrt{p}_k/q_k, b_k=1/\sqrt{p}_k,
  (\sum k/q_k)^2=(\sum a_k b_k)^2\leq (\sum a_k^2)(\sum b_k^2)
    \leq (\sum k^2p_k/q_k^2)(\sum 1/p_k)\leq S_N\cdot T.
Thus A=\sum _{k=2}^{N-1}k/q_k \leq \sqrt{T\cdot S_N}.

6.  Therefore
  S_N \leq 8/p_1 +2\sqrt{T\cdot S_N}+2T,
so setting x=\sqrt{S}_N gives
  x^2-2\sqrt{T}\cdot x-(8/p_1+2T)\leq 0
\Leftrightarrow x\leq \sqrt{T}+\sqrt{3T+8/p_1}.
Thus S_N remains bounded as N\to \infty , and \sum n(n+1)p_n/q_n^2 converges.

core_steps: Introduce partial sums q_n = p_1 + … + p_n and rewrite S_N as Σ (n/q_n)^2 (q_n − q_{n−1}).
Use the inequality (q_n − q_{n−1})/q_{n−1} ≤ 1 to obtain a telescoping bound that reduces S_N to a linear combination of Σ n/q_n, Σ 1/q_n, and a finite constant.
Apply Cauchy–Schwarz to bound Σ n/q_n by √(S_N · T), where T = Σ 1/p_n is finite.
Combine the bounds to get a quadratic inequality S_N ≤ C_0 + 2√(S_N T) + T.
Solve the quadratic inequality to show S_N is bounded, hence the target series converges.

slot1.description: Exact finite constant that comes from isolating the first few terms in the telescoping estimate (written as 5/p_1 in the solution). Any other fixed constant produced by treating finitely many initial indices separately would work.

slot2.description: The point at which the sum is split off before applying the inequality (the solution starts telescoping from n = 2). Any fixed starting index k ≥ 2 would leave the argument intact after adjusting the finite constant.
